topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I watched Sarah glide toward Mr. Henderson, her smile practiced and effortless, as she discussed her recent project successes. Every word she spoke seemed polished, a stark contrast to the frayed edges of my own performance review, which I’d seen posted on the intranet. My jaw tightened, a dull ache settling behind my molars. I gripped my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white, focusing intently on the pattern of the cheap laminate desk. *She always gets the nod,* I thought, the resentment a cold knot in my stomach. I rehearsed a neutral, professional response in my head, but the words caught in my throat, tasting like ash.

[story 2]

I leaned back in my chair, deliberately letting my shoulders slump, trying to project an air of nonchalance I absolutely did not feel. When Mark announced the promotion decision, the air in the conference room seemed to thin. I noticed the way his eyes flicked toward Sarah, a brief, almost imperceptible acknowledgment that felt like a deliberate slight. My hands, resting on the table, began to tremble slightly. I forced a thin, tight smile, the muscles in my face feeling stiff and unnatural. "Congratulations, Mark," I managed, my voice sounding unnervingly flat. Inside, a fierce, simmering energy churned—a deep, internal pressure that made my chest feel constricted. I wanted to lash out, to point out the discrepancies in the evaluation, but I kept my gaze fixed on the water pitcher, refusing to meet anyone’s eyes.

[story 3]

The silence after the announcement hung heavy, thick enough to choke on. I stood up too quickly, the movement jarring my equilibrium. My footsteps echoed too loudly on the polished floor. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush creeping up my cheeks that I desperately tried to mask by focusing on the distant window. Sarah offered a sympathetic nod, a gesture that felt patronizing. I didn't look at her. Instead, I walked toward the exit, my pace brisk and jerky. Every step was an act of contained fury. *This isn't fair,* the thought screamed internally, a raw, painful sound trapped beneath a veneer of practiced composure. I pulled my jacket tighter around myself, the fabric suddenly feeling restrictive, as if I were trying to physically contain the rising tide of indignation.
